methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $200M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Fortera is a Series C-stage climate and energy company founded by Ryan Gilliam that has raised $200M+ to date. The valuation analysis relies on a peer set of three same-sector, same-stage private companies (KoBold Metals at $2.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M), supplemented by reference to a broader comp universe of eight private and one public company in the climate tech space.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total raised ($200M) as a proxy for prior round valuation, applies a typical 1.7x step-up multiplier for Series C advancement, yielding $340M. Peer-median analysis across the three same-stage, same-sector comparables produces a median valuation of $1.0B, with a range spanning $700M to $2.0B. These divergent results reflect meaningful dispersion in frontier-tech valuations at this stage.

Triangulating both methods produces a low estimate of $289M, a median of $670M, and a high of $1.1B. The wide range—reflecting the 1.5x spread between stage-progression and peer-median outputs—points to two material uncertainties: the relevance and stability of early-stage capital raised as a valuation anchor, and the homogeneity of the peer set given the nascent and heterogeneous nature of climate tech sector development.
